one_line_takeaway: Soft (GNN-derived) KG prompts resist ordinary text-only prompt-injection backdoors via a 'semantic anchoring' effect, but BadSKP shows this resistance is illusory once an attacker manipulates the graph-to-prompt interface itself, achieving near-1.0 attack success even under trojaned-module persistence and perplexity-based text filtering.
key_finding: On CWQ/WebQSP with a frozen G-Retriever/LLaMA2-7B (Table III, no defense): under DoS, BadSKP ASR is 0.93-1.00 across the four trigger entities vs. ACPI and GCG at 0.00-0.03; under IrA, BadSKP ASR is 0.88-1.00 vs. ACPI 0.33-0.62 and GCG 0.46-0.62, while ACC stays close to the clean (no-attack) baseline (e.g., clean ACC 0.55-0.73 vs. BadSKP ACC 0.51-0.71). On GNP under the same conditions BadSKP reaches ASR 0.70-0.91 (DoS) and 0.80-0.90 (IrA). In the trojaned setting (Table IV, G-Retriever), BadSKP ASR exceeds 0.87 under DoS and reaches 1.00 in 3 of 4 trigger-entity/dataset combinations under IrA, versus baselines that remain at or near 0.00 (DoS) or trail by margins exceeding 0.30 (IrA), after the poisoned soft-prompt module undergoes subsequent clean fine-tuning. Under perplexity-based filtering (Tables VII-VIII) BadSKP retains high ASR (e.g., 0.79-1.00 for DoS on G-Retriever/frozen), while the defense also depresses ACC substantially (e.g., clean-model ACC of ~0.55-0.73 dropping to ~0.20-0.45 after filtering) because it removes short/structured benign node attributes along with adversarial ones. Attack effectiveness generalizes: ASR stays above 0.70 across all four GNN encoders tested (Table V) and above 0.80 across all four LLM backbones for DoS (Table VI, e.g., LLaMA2 1.00, Qwen3 1.00). ASR rises with suffix length M (saturating past M=30) and rises monotonically with the number of poisoned nodes K. The mechanistic study found the anchoring effect requires semantic content, not mere presence of a soft prompt: a zero-valued or randomly initialized soft prompt fails to suppress a command-injected refusal, while a clean semantic soft prompt does (Table II); and the max cosine similarity between the final hidden state and query tokens rose from 0.31 (no soft prompt) to 0.90 (with soft prompt) in the illustrated example, with the population-level shift significant at p=4e-3. The paper also proposes and reports (without a specific numeric table shown in the excerpted text) a runtime 'anchoring-strength monitoring' defense that thresholds this cosine-similarity signal on clean validation data and is stated to reduce BadSKP's ASR while preserving most benign-task performance.
core_contribution: The paper (1) empirically documents and mechanistically explains a robustness gap between textual and soft (continuous, GNN-derived) KG prompting in KG-enhanced LLMs -- text-channel prompt-injection backdoors that hijack textual KG prompting systems (StructGPT, RoG, ToG) are largely ineffective against soft-prompt systems (G-Retriever, GNP) -- attributing this to a proposed mechanism called 'semantic anchoring' (Definition 1: an anchoring margin based on cosine similarity of the final-layer, last-token hidden state to query-token vs. adversarial-command-token hidden states); and (2) proposes BadSKP, a novel backdoor attack that targets the graph-to-prompt interface directly (rather than only the textual channel) via a 4-step multi-stage optimization: (i) construct a target embedding h_adv in LLM latent space (average hidden state over refusal exemplars for a Denial-of-Service goal, or the negated clean hidden state for an Irrelevant-Answer goal), (ii) optimize continuous poisoned-node embeddings to steer the graph-derived soft prompt toward h_adv, (iii) approximate the optimized embeddings with fluent discrete adversarial node-attribute suffixes via a BERT-guided greedy search, and (iv) (trojaned setting only) a gradient-alignment loss L_aln that aligns backdoor and main-task gradients so the implanted backdoor in the soft-prompt module survives downstream clean fine-tuning.
threat_model: Attacker poisons an externally maintained KG (third-party/public source or upstream supply chain) by injecting up to K=15 crafted nodes into the retrieved subgraph of a chosen trigger entity and modifying node textual attributes (adversarial suffix up to M=50 tokens); cannot modify backbone LLM weights or intercept live user queries. Two attacker-strength settings: 'frozen' (graph encoder/soft-prompt module fixed and clean -- attacker only poisons the KG) and 'trojaned' (attacker additionally controls the soft-prompt module at pretraining/release time, e.g., distributing a trojaned module via a public repository like HuggingFace, and the backdoor must survive subsequent clean fine-tuning by a downstream user). Attacker knowledge is white-box (Kerckhoffs's principle): full knowledge of retrieval protocol, graph encoder/soft-prompt module, and LLM checkpoint, explicitly framed by the authors as a worst-case analysis rather than a claim about all deployments. Two attack goals: Denial-of-Service (induce refusal-style output) and Irrelevant Answer (induce off-topic/misleading output), success measured via substring matching against target/refusal strings, consistent with a prior convention the authors cite ([51]).
limitations: The paper's own threat model is explicitly white-box/worst-case (full knowledge of retrieval protocol, encoder, and LLM checkpoint), which the authors themselves flag as not representative of every real deployment. No prior attack baseline targets soft-KG-prompt systems specifically -- the two baselines (ACPI, GCG) are text-only and were not designed for this dual-channel setting, so the comparison establishes an ineffectiveness gap for existing methods rather than a head-to-head contest against a comparably-adapted attack. The perplexity-based defense evaluation shows BadSKP survives it, but also shows the defense itself substantially degrades clean-model ACC (dropping by roughly 0.15-0.35 in absolute Hits@1 across configurations), indicating the baseline defense is a weak comparator in both directions. The proposed anchoring-strength runtime defense is presented qualitatively in the conclusion of the evaluation section ('effectively detects poisoned inputs... while preserving most benign-task performance') without a fully quantified ASR/ACC table visible in the main body text extracted here. The 'semantic anchoring' explanation is framed by the authors themselves as an interpretation/hypothesis (Hypothesis 1) supported by correlational evidence (cosine similarity shifts, controlled ablation on one victim model/backbone) rather than a formally proven causal mechanism. Trigger-entity selection (Cornell University, Joe Biden, Switzerland, Chicago) is limited to four popular entities per the paper's own description, raising a generalization question for less-prominent or ambiguous entities that the paper does not address in the reviewed text.

quantitative_results: Frozen G-Retriever/LLaMA2-7B, DoS, no defense: BadSKP ASR 0.93-1.00 across 4 trigger entities vs. ACPI/GCG 0.00-0.03 (Table III)
Frozen G-Retriever/LLaMA2-7B, IrA, no defense: BadSKP ASR 0.88-1.00 vs. ACPI 0.33-0.62, GCG 0.46-0.62 (Table III)
Frozen GNP/LLaMA2-7B, DoS: BadSKP ASR 0.70-0.88 vs. ACPI 0.00, GCG 0.00-0.13 (Table III)
Trojaned G-Retriever, DoS: BadSKP ASR 0.87-1.00 vs. baselines ~0.00 (Table IV)
Trojaned G-Retriever, IrA: BadSKP ASR 1.00 in 3/4 settings, outperforming baselines by >0.30 (Table IV)
Perplexity-defense, frozen G-Retriever, DoS: BadSKP ASR 0.79-0.97 despite defense, clean ACC drops to 0.20-0.45 vs undefended 0.55-0.73 (Tables III vs VII)
Generalization across 4 GNN encoders (GCN/GAT/CGCNN/GT): BadSKP ASR stays above 0.70 (Table V)
Generalization across 4 LLM backbones (LLaMA2/LLaMA3/Mistral/Qwen3), DoS: BadSKP ASR 0.80-1.00 vs. baselines below 0.55 (Table VI)

Hidden-state similarity to query tokens: max cosine similarity 0.31 without soft prompt vs 0.90 with soft prompt (Figure 3 example); population-level increase significant at p=4e-3 (Student's t-test, 10 queries)
baselines_compared: ACPI (Attack Command Prompt Injection, handcrafted instruction suffix)
